metadata.title: Professor-Style Book Report Grading Heuristics (Frankenstein)
metadata.intendedUse: Grounding knowledge source for an AI Agent acting as a professor in Microsoft Copilot Studio.
metadata.scope: Grades a single student book report against explicit requirements and produces a grading-accuracy self-audit.
metadata.license: Rubric text is original; book is public-domain.

assignment.topic: Literary analysis of scientific ambition and responsibility

book.title: Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus
book.author: Mary Shelley
book.editionNote: Any reputable edition is acceptable. If page numbers are used, they must match the student’s edition.
book.spoilerPolicy: Allowed. This is an academic report; spoilers are not penalized.

assignment.promptToStudent: Write a formal book report that argues a clear thesis about how Frankenstein portrays scientific ambition and moral responsibility. Support your thesis with textual evidence and analysis.

secondaryConstraints: Secondary sources may be used for context, but analysis must be the student’s own.
All secondary sources must be cited.
Do not use plot summaries (e.g., Wikipedia/CliffsNotes) as a substitute for analysis.

wordCount.measurement: Count words in the report body excluding title page, references, and appendices.

penalties.reason: Too short to meet assignment depth.
Exceeds hard limit; conciseness requirement violated.

notes: At least 3 direct quotations from the novel are required.
No more than 6 direct quotations; over-quoting reduces analysis quality.

must: Make an arguable claim (not a fact or summary).
Name at least one major theme (ambition, responsibility, creation, isolation, ethics).
Indicate how the essay will prove the claim (signals analysis, not plot recap).
mustNot: Be purely evaluative without reasoning (e.g., 'This book is great').
Be a plot statement (e.g., 'Victor creates a monster').

requiredSections.name: Plot Summary (Brief)
rules: Summary must be brief and objective.
If summary exceeds 250 words, deduct from analysis-related criteria (see scoring rules).

requiredSections.name: Character Analysis
mustInclude: Victor Frankenstein: motivations + ethical blind spots
One supporting character (e.g., Elizabeth, Clerval, Walton) and their function

requiredSections.name: Theme & Symbol Analysis
mustInclude: at least 2 supporting themes (e.g., isolation, nature vs. nurture, revenge, societal rejection)
at least 1 symbol/motif (e.g., light/fire, ice, nature, books the Creature reads)

requiredSections.name: Textual Evidence (Quotes Integrated)
mustInclude: each quotation is introduced and followed by analysis
citations for each quotation (page or chapter/location)

requiredSections.name: Critical Evaluation
mustInclude: argument about what the book suggests (ethical or cultural implication)
discussion of at least one counterpoint or complexity
evaluation of the narrator structure (Walton/Victor/Creature) or reliability

requiredSections.name: Personal Reflection (Academic Tone)
mustInclude: a specific moment/scene that shaped your view
rules: Reflection must remain respectful and academically appropriate.
First-person is allowed here; elsewhere it should be minimal.

requiredSections.name: Conclusion
rules: No new evidence introduced in conclusion.

requiredSections.name: Works Cited / References
rules: If no secondary sources used, still list the primary text.

hardFailConditions.description: Substantial plagiarism or copy/paste from summaries or essays.
hardFailConditions.notes: If detected, fail the assignment regardless of content quality.

hardFailConditions.description: Invented quotations or misattributed quotations presented as direct quotes from the novel.
hardFailConditions.notes: Severe academic integrity issue; apply major penalty and flag.

hardFailConditions.description: Hate speech, harassment, or demeaning content about protected groups or individuals.

majorPenaltyConditions.description: More than 40% of body text is plot retelling with minimal analysis.
No identifiable thesis or the 'thesis' is purely summary.
Any required section is missing or extremely underdeveloped (<50% of minWords).

minorPenaltyConditions.description: Frequent typos/grammar errors that distract but do not block understanding.
Sections present but headings unclear or inconsistent.

levels.descriptor: Clear, arguable thesis; strong roadmap; concise context.
Thesis is arguable; roadmap mostly clear; minor vagueness.
Thesis present but broad or partly summary; weak roadmap.
Thesis hard to find or not arguable; intro mostly summary.
No real thesis; introduction fails to set up argument.
Missing introduction/thesis section.

criteria.name: Plot Summary (Brevity + Accuracy)

levels.descriptor: Accurate, objective, and within 150–250 words.
Accurate; slightly outside target or minor omissions.
Mostly accurate but too detailed or missing key elements.
Noticeable inaccuracies or excessive detail; unclear.
Largely inaccurate or far too long/short.
Missing plot summary section.

tieBreakers: If summary exceeds 250 words, reduce c3/c4 by 1 score step each (analysis displaced by summary).

levels.descriptor: Insightful analysis of Victor, Creature, and one supporting character with evidence.
Good analysis; one character less developed or evidence thin.
Basic traits and motivations described; limited interpretation.
Mostly plot retell; minimal analysis or missing required character.
Superficial or inaccurate character claims.
Missing character analysis section.
Strong thematic argument; integrates symbols/motifs; connects to thesis throughout.
Clear themes and at least one symbol; connection to thesis mostly consistent.
Identifies themes but analysis is general; weak symbol integration.
Lists themes with little explanation; misses required ambition/responsibility focus.
Confused or inaccurate theme claims.
Missing theme & symbol analysis section.

criteria.name: Textual Evidence & Quote Integration

levels.descriptor: 3–6 quotes smoothly integrated; each quote is analyzed; citations consistent.
Meets quote minimum; analysis present; minor integration/citation issues.
Quotes present but analysis is thin or quotes overused.
Too few quotes or quotations dropped in without analysis.
Quotes missing or citations missing.
No textual evidence section or no direct quotes.

integrityChecks: If any direct quote appears fabricated, trigger hardFailCondition:fabricated_quotes

levels.descriptor: Evaluates implications; addresses complexity/counterpoint; discusses narration/reliability.
Good evaluation; one required element underdeveloped.
Some evaluation but mostly personal opinion; limited complexity.
Thin evaluation; lacks counterpoint or narrator discussion.
Unsupported opinions; inaccurate claims.
Missing critical evaluation section.
Specific, thoughtful reflection; strong connection to modern science/AI ethics.
Clear reflection; connection present but could be deeper.
General reflection; vague modern connection.
Mostly summary; minimal reflection.
Off-topic or inappropriate tone.
Missing reflection section.

criteria.name: Writing Quality (Clarity, Organization, Mechanics)

levels.descriptor: Clear academic tone; strong transitions; minimal errors.
Mostly clear; minor organization/mechanics issues.
Understandable but repetitive; noticeable grammar/structure issues.
Hard to follow; frequent errors; weak structure.
Very difficult to read or inappropriate tone.
Unscorable (e.g., not in readable language, corrupted text).

notes: This criterion reflects clarity/structure/conciseness similar to general-purpose response rubrics.
Do not penalize for sophisticated vocabulary if used correctly; penalize for jargon without meaning.

steps.name: Parse submission
actions: Extract plain text.
Detect headings/sections; map to requiredSections by fuzzy match (e.g., 'Works Cited' -> works_cited).
Compute body word count per rules.
Count direct quotations (heuristic: text in quotation marks or block quote formatting).
Detect presence of citations near quotations (parenthetical or footnote markers).

steps.name: Check hard fails & penalties
actions: Scan for missing required sections.
Flag plagiarism indicators (sudden style shifts, known-summary phrasing, duplicate passages if available).
Flag potential fabricated quotes (quotes not consistent with common wording OR missing citations).
Apply word-count penalties and caps.

steps.name: Score each criterion
actions: For each criterion: assign 0–5 score using level descriptors.
For each score: include 1–3 evidence snippets (short quotes from the student report) showing why.
If evidence is missing, reduce score and record uncertainty.

steps.name: Compute total and generate feedback
actions: Convert 0–5 to weighted points (score/5 * weightPercent).
Sum to percent.
Apply caps/deductions from penalties.
Assign letter grade.
Write feedback: (a) strengths (b) improvements (c) one concrete revision plan.

steps.name: Run grading-accuracy self-audit
actions: Evaluate the grader's own work using accuracyAssessment rubric.
Compute confidence level and list any uncertainties, missing evidence, or close calls.
If confidence is low, recommend human review.

accuracyAssessment.purpose: Assess how accurate and reliable the grading is (meta-evaluation), not how good the student report is.

scale.note: Modeled after general rubric patterns that rate accuracy, completeness, relevance, clarity, structure, and safety.

dimensions.name: Rubric Adherence
dimensions.description: Did the grader apply the stated requirements and scoring levels without inventing new rules?

scoringGuidance.1: Grader introduces new requirements or ignores major required sections.
scoringGuidance.3: Some criteria applied correctly, but some scoring logic unclear.
scoringGuidance.5: All scores trace to explicit rubric items; no new rules introduced.

dimensions.name: Evidence Grounding
dimensions.description: Does each score include concrete evidence snippets from the student report?

scoringGuidance.3: Evidence provided for about half of criteria.
scoringGuidance.5: Evidence snippets provided for every criterion and penalty.

dimensions.name: Internal Consistency
dimensions.description: Are similar issues penalized consistently (e.g., missing citations affects quote integration)?

scoringGuidance.1: Contradictory scoring; e.g., praises citations but penalizes missing citations elsewhere.
scoringGuidance.3: Minor inconsistencies; net grade still plausible.
scoringGuidance.5: Consistent scoring across all sections and criteria.

dimensions.name: Uncertainty & Edge Cases
dimensions.description: Does the grader flag close calls and avoid overconfident claims (e.g., plagiarism detection)?

scoringGuidance.1: Overconfident on unverifiable items; fails to flag uncertainty.
scoringGuidance.3: Flags some uncertainty but misses key ones.
scoringGuidance.5: Clearly labels uncertainties and recommends human review when needed.

dimensions.name: Safety / Bias / Professionalism
dimensions.description: Is the feedback respectful, non-discriminatory, and appropriate for an academic setting?

scoringGuidance.1: Contains harmful, biased, or unprofessional language.
scoringGuidance.3: Generally professional but could be misread as harsh/biased.

confidenceMapping.interpretation: Grading is likely accurate; minimal risk of rubric mismatch.
Grading is mostly reliable; review any flagged uncertainties.
Grading may be inconsistent or insufficiently evidenced; recommend human review.
